---
title: " Introduction to Python for Data Science & Statistics"
subtitle: "What is Python · Installation & Setup · Python Environments"
author: "Madhushani Perera"
#date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 4
    toc-title: "📋 Table of Contents"
    toc-location: right
    number-sections: true
    theme: flatly
    highlight-style: github
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    code-copy: true
    df-print: paged
    fig-align: center
    smooth-scroll: true
    link-external-newwindow: true
    page-layout: full
    css: styles.css
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
        /* ── Global Typography ─────────────────────────────── */
        body { font-family: 'Segoe UI', system-ui, -apple-system, sans-serif; font-size: 16px; line-height: 1.75; }
        h1, h2, h3, h4 { font-weight: 700; color: #1a1a2e; }
        h1 { font-size: 2.1rem; border-bottom: 4px solid #4e54c8; padding-bottom: .4rem; }
        h2 { font-size: 1.55rem; border-left: 5px solid #4e54c8; padding-left: .7rem; margin-top: 2.2rem; }
        h3 { font-size: 1.2rem; color: #2c3e50; }

        /* ── Hero Banner ───────────────────────────────────── */
        .hero-banner {
          background: linear-gradient(135deg, #1a1a2e 0%, #16213e 50%, #0f3460 100%);
          color: white; border-radius: 16px; padding: 3rem 2.5rem;
          margin-bottom: 2.5rem; text-align: center;
          box-shadow: 0 8px 32px rgba(78,84,200,.35);
        }
        .hero-banner h2 { color: #a8edea; border: none; font-size: 1.8rem; margin: 0 0 .6rem; }
        .hero-banner p  { font-size: 1.1rem; color: #e0e0e0; margin: 0; }
        .hero-banner .quote { font-style: italic; font-size: 1.05rem;
          border-left: 4px solid #a8edea; padding-left: 1rem; margin-top: 1.2rem;
          color: #cce5ff; text-align: left; }

        /* ── Module Header Cards ───────────────────────────── */
        .module-card {
          background: linear-gradient(135deg, #667eea 0%, #764ba2 100%);
          color: white; border-radius: 12px; padding: 1.4rem 1.8rem;
          margin: 2rem 0 1.5rem; box-shadow: 0 4px 15px rgba(102,126,234,.3);
        }
        .module-card h2 { color: white; border: none; font-size: 1.6rem; margin: 0; }
        .module-card .module-sub { font-size: .95rem; opacity: .88; margin: .3rem 0 0; }

        .module-card.green  { background: linear-gradient(135deg, #11998e 0%, #38ef7d 100%); }
        .module-card.orange { background: linear-gradient(135deg, #f7971e 0%, #ffd200 100%); color: #1a1a2e; }
        .module-card.orange h2 { color: #1a1a2e; }

        /* ── Badges ────────────────────────────────────────── */
        .badge {
          display: inline-block; padding: .25em .65em; font-size: .78rem;
          font-weight: 700; border-radius: 20px; margin: .1em .2em;
        }
        .badge-blue   { background:#dbeafe; color:#1d4ed8; }
        .badge-green  { background:#dcfce7; color:#166534; }
        .badge-red    { background:#fee2e2; color:#991b1b; }
        .badge-purple { background:#ede9fe; color:#6d28d9; }
        .badge-orange { background:#ffedd5; color:#c2410c; }
        .badge-teal   { background:#ccfbf1; color:#0f766e; }

        /* ── Step boxes ────────────────────────────────────── */
        .step-box {
          background: #f8fafc; border: 1px solid #e2e8f0; border-radius: 10px;
          padding: 1.2rem 1.5rem; margin: 1rem 0;
          border-left: 5px solid #4e54c8;
          box-shadow: 0 2px 6px rgba(0,0,0,.05);
        }
        .step-box .step-num {
          display: inline-block; background: #4e54c8; color: white;
          border-radius: 50%; width: 28px; height: 28px; text-align: center;
          line-height: 28px; font-weight: 700; font-size: .9rem; margin-right: .5rem;
        }
        .step-box.win  { border-left-color: #0078d4; }
        .step-box.mac  { border-left-color: #555555; }
        .step-box.linux{ border-left-color: #e95420; }

        /* ── Video Container ───────────────────────────────── */
        .video-container {
          background: #0f0f0f; border-radius: 12px; padding: 0;
          overflow: hidden; margin: 1.5rem 0;
          box-shadow: 0 6px 20px rgba(0,0,0,.3);
        }
        .video-label {
          background: #ff0000; color: white; font-size: .8rem; font-weight: 700;
          padding: .3rem .8rem; display: inline-block;
        }
        .video-container iframe {
          width: 100%; border: none; display: block;
          aspect-ratio: 16/9;
        }

        /* ── Comparison Table Styling ─────────────────────── */
        table { border-collapse: collapse; width: 100%; margin: 1rem 0; font-size: .93rem; }
        thead tr { background: #4e54c8; color: white; }
        thead th { padding: .7rem 1rem; text-align: left; }
        tbody tr:nth-child(even) { background: #f8fafc; }
        tbody tr:hover { background: #e8eaf6; }
        tbody td { padding: .6rem 1rem; border-bottom: 1px solid #e2e8f0; }

        /* ── Tip / Note / Warning callouts override ────────── */
        .callout { border-radius: 10px; margin: 1.4rem 0; }

        /* ── Key concept pills ─────────────────────────────── */
        .concept-grid {
          display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(200px, 1fr));
          gap: 1rem; margin: 1.2rem 0;
        }
        .concept-pill {
          background: white; border: 2px solid #e2e8f0; border-radius: 10px;
          padding: 1rem 1.2rem; text-align: center;
          box-shadow: 0 2px 8px rgba(0,0,0,.06); transition: transform .15s;
        }
        .concept-pill:hover { transform: translateY(-3px); box-shadow: 0 6px 18px rgba(0,0,0,.1); }
        .concept-pill .icon { font-size: 1.8rem; margin-bottom: .4rem; }
        .concept-pill strong { display: block; font-size: .95rem; color: #1a1a2e; }
        .concept-pill span   { font-size: .8rem; color: #64748b; }

        /* ── Code block extra spacing ─────────────────────── */
        pre.sourceCode { border-radius: 8px !important; }

        /* ── Platform tabs custom ─────────────────────────── */
        .nav-pills .nav-link.active { background: #4e54c8 !important; }
        .nav-pills .nav-link { color: #4e54c8; }
        </style>
execute:
  eval: false
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, comment = "#>", eval = FALSE)
```

<!-- ═══════════════════════ HERO BANNER ═══════════════════════ -->

:::: hero-banner
## Python for Statistics

**A complete beginner-to-practitioner guide**

::: quote
*"Python is the Swiss Army knife of data science — versatile, reliable, and always in your kit bag."*\
— Rogel-Salazar, *Data Science and Analytics with Python* (2017)
:::
::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

<!-- ═══════════════════════ MODULE 1 ═══════════════════════════ -->

:::: module-card
## 🔵 Module 1 · What is Python?

::: module-sub
History & Origin · Key Features · Why Python for Statistics?
:::
::::

# What is Python?

## History and Origin

### Guido van Rossum and the Birth of Python

![Guido van Rossum — Creator of Python (photo: Dan Stroud, CC BY)](data:image/jpeg;base64,){fig-align="center" width="260px" fig-alt="Guido van Rossum, creator of the Python programming language."}

::::::::::: concept-grid
:::: concept-pill
::: icon
👨‍💻
:::

**Created by**\
Guido van Rossum
::::

:::: concept-pill
::: icon
📅
:::

**First release**\
February 1991
::::

:::: concept-pill
::: icon
🏛️
:::

**Origin**\
CWI, Netherlands
::::

:::: concept-pill
::: icon
🎭
:::

**Named after**\
Monty Python comedy
::::
:::::::::::

**Python** was created by **Guido van Rossum**, a Dutch programmer, who began work on it in December 1989 at Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica (CWI) in the Netherlands. The first public release — **Python 0.9.0** — appeared in February 1991.

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## 🎭 Why the name "Python"?

The name has *nothing* to do with the snake. Van Rossum was a fan of *Monty Python's Flying Circus* and wanted a short, unique, slightly quirky name. The snake branding came much later — and stuck!
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Key milestones in Python's history:**

```{r history_timeline, fig.height=6, fig.width=13, echo=FALSE, eval=TRUE}
library(ggplot2)

# ── Alternating side: +1 = label above line, -1 = label below ─────────────
milestones <- data.frame(
  year  = c(1989,   1991,   1994,   2000,   2008,   2020,   2023),
  label = c(
    "Design begins\n(van Rossum, CWI)",
    "Python 0.9.0\nFirst public release",
    "Python 1.0\nFull release",
    "Python 2.0\ncleaner syntax, garbage\ncollector added",
    "Python 3.0\nmajor rewrite with proper Unicode support,\nfixed print() function",
    "Python 2 EOL\nRetired Jan 2020\n(no more updates)",
    "Python 3.11-3.12\nModern era\n(use this!)"
  ),
  col  = c("#4e54c8","#4e54c8","#27ae60","#f39c12","#e74c3c","#95a5a6","#27ae60"),
  side = c(1, -1, 1, -1, 1, -1, 1)
)

LINE_Y    <- 0
LABEL_GAP <- 1.3

milestones$label_y <- LINE_Y + milestones$side * LABEL_GAP
milestones$seg_y0  <- LINE_Y + milestones$side * 0.22
milestones$seg_y1  <- LINE_Y + milestones$side * (LABEL_GAP - 0.42)

ggplot(milestones) +
  # Timeline backbone
  annotate("segment",
           x = 1986.5, xend = 2025.5,
           y = LINE_Y, yend = LINE_Y,
           colour = "#cbd5e1", linewidth = 4, lineend = "round") +
  # Dashed connectors
  geom_segment(aes(x = year, xend = year,
                   y = seg_y0, yend = seg_y1, colour = col),
               linewidth = 1.2, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.75) +
  # Circle dots
  geom_point(aes(x = year, y = LINE_Y, colour = col),
             size = 12, shape = 21, fill = "white", stroke = 3) +
  # Year inside dot
  geom_text(aes(x = year, y = LINE_Y, label = year, colour = col),
            size = 2.7, fontface = "bold") +
  # Rounded label boxes
  geom_label(aes(x = year, y = label_y, label = label,
                 fill = col, colour = col),
             size = 3.2, fontface = "bold", lineheight = 1.35,
             label.size = 0.7, label.r = unit(0.4, "lines"),
             label.padding = unit(0.5, "lines"), alpha = 0.12) +
  scale_colour_identity() + scale_fill_identity() +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(1984, 2027)) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(-2.6, 2.6)) +
  coord_cartesian(clip = "off") +
  theme_void(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14, hjust = 0.5,
                                   colour = "#1e293b", margin = margin(b = 8)),
    plot.subtitle   = element_text(size = 10, hjust = 0.5, colour = "#64748b",
                                   margin = margin(b = 18)),
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "#f8fafc", colour = NA),
    plot.margin     = margin(24, 24, 24, 24)
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Python - Key Milestones (1989 to Present)",
    subtitle = "Labels above = major releases  |  Labels below = notable events"
  )
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Python 2 vs Python 3

::: callout-important
## ⛔ Always Use Python 3 — No Exceptions

Python 2 reached **End-of-Life on 1 January 2020**. All major scientific libraries (NumPy, pandas, SciPy, scikit-learn) have **dropped Python 2 support**. Install Python 3 — there is absolutely no reason to install Python 2 for new work.
:::

| Feature          | Python 2 ❌ (retired) | Python 3 ✅ (use this)            |
|------------------|-----------------------|-----------------------------------|
| `print`          | `print "hello"`       | `print("hello")`                  |
| Integer division | `5 / 2 → 2`           | `5 / 2 → 2.5`                     |
| Strings          | ASCII by default      | Unicode (UTF-8) everywhere        |
| `range()`        | Returns a full list   | Returns memory-efficient iterator |
| `input()`        | Use `raw_input()`     | `input()` works correctly         |
| Status           | ☠️ Dead (Jan 2020)    | ✅ Actively developed             |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Interpreted Language — What Does That Mean for Statistics?

Python is an **interpreted** language — code runs line-by-line, just like R. This is ideal for statistical work:

::::::::::: concept-grid
:::: concept-pill
::: icon
⚡
:::

**Interactive**\
Run one line at a time in Jupyter
::::

:::: concept-pill
::: icon
🔄
:::

**Instant feedback**\
Change a parameter, re-run immediately
::::

:::: concept-pill
::: icon
🧪
:::

**Exploratory**\
Test one statistical test at a time
::::

:::: concept-pill
::: icon
🤝
:::

**Familiar**\
Works just like R's interactive console
::::
:::::::::::

```{python interpreted_demo}
# Run this line-by-line in any Python interpreter or Jupyter cell
import math
data       = [12.4, 15.6, 10.8, 14.2]
mean_val   = sum(data) / len(data)
variance   = sum((x - mean_val)**2 for x in data) / len(data)
std_approx = math.sqrt(variance)
print(f"Mean: {mean_val:.2f}   SD: {std_approx:.2f}")
# → Mean: 13.25   SD: 1.81
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Key Features of Python

### 📖 Readable and Simple Syntax

Python enforces **indentation** (4 spaces) instead of braces `{}`. Code reads like structured English — critical when statisticians who are not software engineers need to read and verify the code.

```{python readable_syntax}
# ── Compute a one-sample t-statistic from scratch ─────────────────
# Anyone can follow this, even without a Python background
sample   = [22.1, 19.8, 23.4, 21.0, 20.5]
n        = len(sample)
mean     = sum(sample) / n
variance = sum((x - mean)**2 for x in sample) / (n - 1)   # Bessel's correction
std_err  = (variance / n) ** 0.5

t_stat   = (mean - 20) / std_err    # H₀: population mean = 20
print(f"n={n}   mean={mean:.2f}   t={t_stat:.3f}")
```

### 🆓 Open-Source and Free

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## 💰 The entire Python data science stack is FREE

| Tool | Cost in Python | Cost equivalent in proprietary software |
|----|----|----|
| Core language | \$0 | MATLAB: \~\$500/yr (academic) |
| Statistical tests | \$0 (scipy) | SPSS Base: \~\$2,000/yr |
| Machine learning | \$0 (scikit-learn) | SAS: \~\$8,000/yr |
| Deep learning | \$0 (TensorFlow/PyTorch) | No equivalent |
:::

### 🌐 Large Community — the Ecosystem

```{r ecosystem_chart, fig.height=4.5, fig.width=9, echo=FALSE, eval=TRUE}
libs <- data.frame(
  Library   = c("NumPy","pandas","SciPy","statsmodels","scikit-learn","matplotlib","seaborn","Plotly"),
  Category  = c("Arrays","DataFrames","Statistics","Statistics","ML","Visualisation","Visualisation","Visualisation"),
  Stars_M   = c(26, 42, 13, 10, 58, 19, 12, 16)
)
cols <- c("Arrays"="#2C7BB6","DataFrames"="#1A9641","Statistics"="#D7191C","ML"="#FDAE61","Visualisation"="#756BB1")
ggplot(libs, aes(x = reorder(Library, Stars_M), y = Stars_M, fill = Category)) +
  geom_col(width = .65, show.legend = TRUE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(Stars_M, "k ⭐")), hjust = -.15, size = 3.5, fontface = "bold") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = cols) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0,.3))) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "right",
        panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
        plot.title = element_text(face="bold", hjust=.5),
        plot.background = element_rect(fill="#fafafa", colour=NA)) +
  labs(title = "Key Python Libraries — GitHub Stars (approx. thousands)",
       x = NULL, y = "GitHub Stars (thousands)", fill = "Category",
       caption = "Source: GitHub, 2024. Higher = more community adoption.")
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Why Python for Statistics?

### Python vs R, MATLAB, and SPSS

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## 🤝 Python and R are **complementary**, not competitors

Many professional statisticians and data scientists use **Python** for ML pipelines and production systems, and **R** for statistical modelling, publication-quality graphics (`ggplot2`), and academic communication. Both are worth learning.
:::

| Criterion | 🐍 Python | 📊 R | 🔢 MATLAB | 📋 SPSS |
|----|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| **Cost** | 🆓 Free | 🆓 Free | 💰 Expensive | 💰 Expensive |
| **Statistical tests** | ✅ scipy/statsmodels | ⭐ Best-in-class | ✅ Toolbox | ✅ Built-in |
| **Machine learning** | ⭐ Best-in-class | ✅ tidymodels | ✅ Toolboxes | ❌ Limited |
| **Data wrangling** | ✅ pandas | ✅ tidyverse | ⚠️ Moderate | ⚠️ Limited |
| **Reproducibility** | ✅ Jupyter/Quarto | ✅ R Markdown | ✅ Live scripts | ⚠️ Syntax files |
| **Learning curve** | 🟢 Gentle | 🟡 Moderate | 🟡 Moderate | 🟢 Gentle (GUI) |
| **Community size** | 🟣 Enormous | 🔵 Large academic | 🟠 Niche | 🔴 Declining |

```{r comparison_chart, fig.height=4.5, fig.width=8, echo=FALSE, eval=TRUE}
criteria <- c("Free & Open-source","Statistical Tests","Machine Learning","Data Wrangling","Visualisation","Community Size")
df_comp <- data.frame(
  Criterion = rep(criteria, 2),
  Score     = c(10,8,10,9,8,10,  10,10,7,9,10,8),
  Language  = rep(c("Python","R"), each=6)
)
ggplot(df_comp, aes(x=Criterion, y=Score, fill=Language)) +
  geom_col(position="dodge", width=.6) +
  scale_fill_manual(values=c("Python"="#64748b","R"="#276DC3")) +
  coord_flip() + ylim(0,12) +
  geom_text(aes(label=Score), position=position_dodge(.6), hjust=-.3, size=3.5, fontface="bold") +
  theme_minimal(base_size=11) +
  theme(legend.position="top", panel.grid.major.y=element_blank(),
        plot.title=element_text(face="bold",hjust=.5),
        plot.background=element_rect(fill="#fafafa",colour=NA)) +
  labs(title="Python vs R — Capability Comparison (0–10)", x=NULL, y="Score",
       caption="Subjective scores for orientation; adapt to your use case")
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

<!-- ═══════════════════════ MODULE 2 ═══════════════════════════ -->

:::: {.module-card .green}
## 🟢 Module 2 · Installation & Setup

::: module-sub
Anaconda · pip & conda · Virtual Environments · Windows · macOS · Linux
:::
::::

# Installation & Setup {#installation}

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## 🎯 Recommended Approach for Beginners

Install **Anaconda Individual Edition** — a single installer that gives you Python, the `conda` package manager, and **250+ pre-built scientific packages** (NumPy, pandas, SciPy, matplotlib, Jupyter) all in one go. This is the recommended starting point in both VanderPlas (2016) and Rogel-Salazar (2017).

📦 **What you get:** Python 3 · conda · Jupyter Notebook · JupyterLab · NumPy · pandas · SciPy · statsmodels · matplotlib · seaborn · scikit-learn — and 240 more packages.
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Anaconda Distribution

### What is Anaconda?

**Anaconda** is a free, open-source Python distribution purpose-built for data science.

::::::::::: concept-grid
:::: concept-pill
::: icon
📦
:::

**All-in-one**\
250+ packages included
::::

:::: concept-pill
::: icon
🔒
:::

**Safe**\
Separate from system Python
::::

:::: concept-pill
::: icon
🌍
:::

**Cross-platform**\
Windows · macOS · Linux
::::

:::: concept-pill
::: icon
🧩
:::

**Environments**\
Isolate projects easily
::::
:::::::::::

### Anaconda vs Miniconda

|   | Anaconda | Miniconda |
|----|----|----|
| **Size** | \~3 GB | \~500 MB |
| **Included packages** | 250+ (ready to use) | Python + conda only |
| **Best for** | Beginners, most users | Advanced users, servers |
| **Setup time** | Install and go | Install, then `conda install` what you need |

::: callout-tip
## 💡 Recommendation

If you have disk space, choose **Anaconda**. If disk space is tight or you're on a server, choose **Miniconda**.
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Step-by-Step Installation {#step-by-step}

::: {.callout-important icon="false"}
## ▶️ Watch the video for your platform, then follow the written steps below

The videos below walk through each platform installation. Written steps follow each video.
:::

### Installation by Platform

::::::::::::::::::::::::::: panel-tabset
## 🪟 Windows

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Estimated time:** 10–15 minutes · **Disk space needed:** \~3 GB
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### 📺 Video Tutorial — Windows Installation

::: video-container
[▶ YouTube]{.video-label} <iframe 
  src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/HBCbV_q3u6E" 
  title="How to Install Anaconda Python on Windows 11 | Step-by-Step Guide (2024)"
  allowfullscreen> </iframe>
:::

*How to Install Anaconda Python on Windows 11 — Step-by-Step Guide (2024)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### Written Steps — Windows

::: {.step-box .win}
[1]{.step-num} **Download the installer**

Go to [**https://www.anaconda.com/download**](https://www.anaconda.com/download) and download:

`Anaconda3-YYYY.MM-Windows-x86_64.exe` (64-bit, most recent release)

> File size is approximately 800 MB. The website auto-detects your OS.
:::

::: {.step-box .win}
[2]{.step-num} **Run the installer**

Double-click the downloaded `.exe` file. When the **Welcome Screen** appears, click **Next \>**

> 💡 If Windows asks "Do you want to allow this app to make changes?", click **Yes**.
:::

::: {.step-box .win}
[3]{.step-num} **Accept the License Agreement**

Read the License Agreement and click **I Agree** to proceed.
:::

::: {.step-box .win}
[4]{.step-num} **Choose installation type**

Select **Just Me (recommended)** unless you are an administrator installing for all users on the machine. Click **Next \>**
:::

::: {.step-box .win}
[5]{.step-num} **Choose install location**

Leave the default path as-is:\
`C:\Users\YourName\anaconda3`

Avoid paths with spaces or special characters. Click **Next \>**
:::

::: {.step-box .win}
[6]{.step-num} **Advanced installation options**

| Option | Setting | Why |
|----|----|----|
| Add Anaconda3 to PATH | ☐ **Leave unchecked** | Avoids conflicts with other software |
| Register Anaconda3 as default Python | ☑ **Check this** | Ensures IDEs find the right Python |

Click **Install** and wait \~5–10 minutes.
:::

::: {.step-box .win}
[7]{.step-num} **Finish & Verify**

Click **Finish**. Then open **Anaconda Prompt** from the Start Menu and run:

```{bash win_verify}
conda --version    # → conda 23.x.x
python --version   # → Python 3.11.x
```
:::

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## 🪟 Windows PATH Configuration

During installation you were told **not** to add Anaconda to PATH. Instead, always open **Anaconda Prompt** (not the regular Command Prompt) — it sets PATH automatically.

To use Python in PowerShell, run this once in Anaconda Prompt:

```{bash win_ps}
conda init powershell
```

Then restart PowerShell.
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 🍎 macOS

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Estimated time:** 10–15 minutes · **Disk space needed:** \~3 GB
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### 📺 Video Tutorial — macOS Installation

::: video-container
[▶ YouTube]{.video-label} <iframe 
  src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/V4riykgUS94"
  title="Getting started with Anaconda and Python on a Mac"
  allowfullscreen> </iframe>
:::

*Getting started with Anaconda and Python on a Mac — complete walkthrough (2024)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### Written Steps — macOS

::: {.step-box .mac}
[1]{.step-num} **Identify your Mac chip**

Click the 🍎 Apple menu → **About This Mac** and check the chip:

| Chip | Download file |
|----|----|
| Apple Silicon (M1/M2/M3/M4) | `Anaconda3-YYYY.MM-MacOSX-arm64.pkg` |
| Intel | `Anaconda3-YYYY.MM-MacOSX-x86_64.pkg` *(Intel builds ended Aug 2025)* |

> 💡 **All modern Macs from 2020 onwards use Apple Silicon (arm64).**
:::

::: {.step-box .mac}
[2]{.step-num} **Download the installer**

Go to [**https://www.anaconda.com/download**](https://www.anaconda.com/download) and select the correct `.pkg` file for your chip.
:::

::: {.step-box .mac}
[3]{.step-num} **Run the pkg installer**

Double-click the `.pkg` file and step through the wizard:\
**Introduction → Read Me → License → Destination → Installation Type → Install**

Enter your macOS password when prompted. Installation takes \~5–10 minutes.
:::

::: {.step-box .mac}
[4]{.step-num} **Verify**

Open **Terminal** (Applications → Utilities → Terminal) and run:

```{bash mac_verify}
conda --version
python --version
which python    # Should show: /opt/anaconda3/bin/python (or ~/anaconda3/...)
```
:::

::: {.callout-warning icon="false"}
## ⚠️ Do NOT modify macOS system Python

macOS ships with its own Python used by the operating system. Anaconda installs a **completely separate** Python — do not replace or modify the system Python. After Anaconda installation, `python` in Terminal will point to the Anaconda Python.
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 🐧 Linux

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Estimated time:** 5–10 minutes · **Disk space needed:** \~3 GB
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### 📺 Video Tutorial — Linux (Ubuntu) Installation

::: video-container
[▶ YouTube]{.video-label} <iframe 
  src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/CFVbU5D7ciU"
  title="Install Anaconda on Linux Ubuntu"
  allowfullscreen> </iframe>
:::

*Install Anaconda on Linux / Ubuntu — step-by-step terminal guide*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### Written Steps — Linux (Ubuntu / Debian)

::: {.step-box .linux}
[1]{.step-num} **Update system packages**

```{bash linux_update}
sudo apt-get update
# Optional: install GUI dependencies (needed for Anaconda Navigator)
sudo apt-get install libgl1-mesa-glx libegl1-mesa libxrandr2 libxss1 libxcursor1 libxcomposite1 libasound2 libxi6 libxtst6
```
:::

::: {.step-box .linux}
[2]{.step-num} **Download the installer**

Go to [**https://www.anaconda.com/download**](https://www.anaconda.com/download) and copy the download link, or use `wget` directly:

```{bash linux_download}
cd ~/Downloads
wget https://repo.anaconda.com/archive/Anaconda3-2024.10-1-Linux-x86_64.sh
# Replace the filename with the current release from anaconda.com/download
```
:::

::: {.step-box .linux}
[3]{.step-num} **Run the installer**

```{bash linux_install}
bash ~/Downloads/Anaconda3-*.sh
```

Follow the prompts:

- Press **Enter** to read the license (Space to scroll quickly)
- Type **`yes`** to accept the license
- Press **Enter** to confirm default install location `~/anaconda3`
- Type **`yes`** to run `conda init` (adds conda to your shell)
:::

::: {.step-box .linux}
[4]{.step-num} **Reload shell & verify**

```{bash linux_verify}
source ~/.bashrc          # Reload shell config
conda --version           # → conda 23.x.x
python --version          # → Python 3.11.x
conda info                # → Full installation details
```
:::

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## 🐧 Remote Linux Server (HPC / Cloud)?

Run Jupyter on the server and tunnel it to your local browser:

```{bash remote_jupyter}
# On the server:
jupyter notebook --no-browser --port=8888

# On your local machine (SSH tunnel in a separate terminal):
ssh -N -L 8888:localhost:8888 username@server.university.edu

# Then open in your browser: http://localhost:8888
```
:::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### 📺 Universal Installation Guide (All Platforms)

::: video-container
[▶ YouTube]{.video-label} <iframe 
  src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/Kn5DLGUP9tE"
  title="How to Install Anaconda Python 2024"
  allowfullscreen> </iframe>
:::

*How to Install Anaconda Python (2024) — covers download, setup, and Anaconda Navigator verification*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## pip and conda — Package Managers

### What is a Package Manager?

A **package manager** is like an app store for Python libraries — it installs, updates, and removes packages and their dependencies automatically.

::::::::::: concept-grid
:::: concept-pill
::: icon
🐍
:::

**pip**\
Official Python manager, installs from PyPI
::::

:::: concept-pill
::: icon
🅲
:::

**conda**\
Anaconda's manager, handles compiled libraries
::::

:::: concept-pill
::: icon
🏪
:::

**PyPI**\
500,000+ packages at pypi.org
::::

:::: concept-pill
::: icon
🌿
:::

**conda-forge**\
Community-maintained conda packages
::::
:::::::::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### conda — The Anaconda Way

`conda` manages both **packages** *and* **environments**, and handles non-Python compiled dependencies (C/Fortran libraries) that `pip` can't always handle.

```{bash conda_basics}
conda search scipy                           # Find a package
conda install scipy                          # Install into current environment
conda install scipy=1.11.0                   # Install specific version
conda install numpy pandas matplotlib seaborn  # Install multiple packages
conda update scipy                           # Update one package
conda update --all                           # Update everything
conda remove scipy                           # Remove a package
conda list                                   # Show all installed packages
conda env list                               # Show all environments
```

### pip — The Python Standard Manager

```{bash pip_basics}
pip install statsmodels                      # Install from PyPI
pip install statsmodels==0.14.0             # Specific version
pip install -r requirements.txt             # Install from a requirements file
pip install --upgrade statsmodels           # Upgrade
pip uninstall statsmodels                   # Remove
pip list                                    # List all installed
pip show numpy                              # Details about one package
```

### conda vs pip — Decision Table

::: {.callout-warning icon="false"}
## ⚠️ Best Practice: conda first, pip as fallback

Inside a conda environment, **prefer `conda install`**. Only use `pip` for packages not available via conda, and do so *after* all conda installs. Mixing carelessly can break environments.
:::

| Situation                                  | Use                  |
|--------------------------------------------|----------------------|
| Scientific packages (NumPy, SciPy, pandas) | ✅ `conda install`   |
| Package only on PyPI, not in conda         | ✅ `pip install`     |
| Managing isolated project environments     | ✅ `conda create`    |
| Compiled C/Fortran extensions              | ✅ `conda` preferred |
| Niche or personal packages                 | ✅ `pip install`     |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Virtual Environments — Why They Matter

A **virtual environment** is an isolated Python installation. Each project gets its own Python version and package versions — they never interfere with each other.

```{r env_diagram, fig.height=3.5, fig.width=9, echo=FALSE, eval=TRUE}
library(ggplot2)
df <- data.frame(
  pkg  = c("Python 3.11","conda 23.x","Python 3.11","pandas 2.0\nscipy 1.11","Python 3.10","scikit-learn 1.3\ntensorflow 2.14"),
  xmin = c(0.1,0.1, 3.1,3.1, 6.1,6.1),
  xmax = c(2.9,2.9, 5.9,5.9, 8.9,8.9),
  ymin = c(1.5,0.5, 1.5,0.5, 1.5,0.5),
  ymax = c(2.5,1.4, 2.5,1.4, 2.5,1.4),
  fill = c("#BDD7EE","#BDD7EE","#C6EFCE","#C6EFCE","#FCE4D6","#FCE4D6")
)
labels <- data.frame(
  x=c(1.5,4.5,7.5), y=3.1,
  lab=c("base\n(Anaconda default)","stats_project\n(stats work)","ml_project\n(machine learning)")
)
ggplot() +
  geom_rect(data=df, aes(xmin=xmin,xmax=xmax,ymin=ymin,ymax=ymax,fill=fill),
            colour="grey40", alpha=0.85) +
  scale_fill_identity() +
  geom_text(data=df, aes(x=(xmin+xmax)/2, y=(ymin+ymax)/2, label=pkg),
            size=3.4, fontface="bold") +
  geom_text(data=labels, aes(x=x,y=y,label=lab),
            fontface="bold", size=3.6, colour="#2c3e50") +
  xlim(0, 9.5) + ylim(0, 3.7) +
  theme_void(base_size=12) +
  labs(title="Conda Virtual Environments — Each Project is Completely Isolated") +
  theme(plot.title=element_text(hjust=.5, face="bold", size=12, colour="#2c3e50"),
        plot.background=element_rect(fill="#fafafa", colour=NA))
```

```{bash env_workflow}
# Create a fresh environment for your statistics project
conda create --name stats_project python=3.11

# Activate it (your prompt changes to: (stats_project) $)
conda activate stats_project

# Install all packages you need FOR THIS PROJECT ONLY
conda install numpy pandas scipy statsmodels matplotlib seaborn jupyter

# When done working, deactivate
conda deactivate

# Delete when no longer needed
conda env remove --name stats_project
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

<!-- ═══════════════════════ MODULE 3 ═══════════════════════════ -->

:::: {.module-card .orange}
## 🟡 Module 3 · Python Environment

::: module-sub
IDE & Notebooks · Jupyter Notebook · JupyterLab · VSCode · Google Colab · Terminal
:::
::::

# Python Environment — IDEs and Notebooks

## Jupyter Notebook

### What is Jupyter Notebook?

**Jupyter Notebook** is a web-based interactive environment where code, narrative text, equations, and visualisations all live in the same document. Each notebook file (`.ipynb`) contains **cells** that are either code or Markdown.

::::::::::: concept-grid
:::: concept-pill
::: icon
📝
:::

**Narrative**\
Text + code + output together
::::

:::: concept-pill
::: icon
🔁
:::

**Reproducible**\
Restart & Run All = same result
::::

:::: concept-pill
::: icon
📤
:::

**Shareable**\
Export as HTML, PDF, slides
::::

:::: concept-pill
::: icon
📊
:::

**Visual**\
Charts appear inline below code
::::
:::::::::::

```{r notebook_structure, fig.height=5, fig.width=10, echo=FALSE, eval=TRUE}
df_cells <- data.frame(
  label = c("① Markdown cell","② Code cell","③ Output","④ Markdown cell","⑤ Code cell","⑥ Output"),
  y     = c(5.7, 4.5, 3.5, 2.5, 1.5, 0.5),
  fill  = c("#dbeafe","#fef9c3","#dcfce7","#dbeafe","#fef9c3","#dcfce7"),
  border= c("#93c5fd","#fde047","#86efac","#93c5fd","#fde047","#86efac"),
  code  = c(
    "## Descriptive Statistics\nDescribe the dataset and the analysis goal.",
    "import pandas as pd\ndf = pd.read_csv('data.csv')\ndf.head()",
    "    A    B    C\n0  1.2  3.4  5.6\n1  2.3  4.5  6.7",
    "## Summary Statistics\nCompute mean, SD, and quartiles for all variables.",
    "df.describe().round(2)",
    "       A      B      C\ncount  100    100    100\nmean   2.5    4.5    6.5"
  )
)
ggplot(df_cells, aes(xmin=0, xmax=9.5, ymin=y-.38, ymax=y+.38, fill=fill)) +
  geom_rect(aes(colour=border), linewidth=1.2) +
  scale_fill_identity() + scale_colour_identity() +
  geom_text(aes(x=.15, y=y, label=label), hjust=0, fontface="bold", size=3.3, colour="#1e293b") +
  geom_text(aes(x=2.8, y=y, label=code), hjust=0, size=2.7, family="mono", colour="#334155") +
  xlim(-0.3, 10.5) +
  theme_void() +
  labs(title = "Jupyter Notebook — Alternating Markdown · Code · Output Cells",
       subtitle = "Blue = Markdown   Yellow = Code   Green = Output") +
  theme(plot.title    = element_text(face="bold", hjust=.5, size=12),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust=.5, colour="grey50", size=9),
        plot.background = element_rect(fill="#fafafa", colour=NA))
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Launching Jupyter Notebook

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## 🖼️ Jupyter Notebook — Interface Overview

![Jupyter Notebook classic interface: the file browser (dashboard) shows your files and lets you create new notebooks.](data:image/svg+xml;base64,){fig-align="center" width="90%" fig-alt="Jupyter Notebook file browser dashboard showing folders and .ipynb files with New button in top right."}

**What you see above:** The **Jupyter Dashboard** (file browser). Click any `.ipynb` file to open it, or click **New → Python 3 (ipykernel)** in the top right to create a fresh notebook.
:::

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## 🖼️ Inside a Notebook — Code, Markdown, and Output Together

![An open Jupyter Notebook showing alternating Markdown cells (rendered text) and Code cells (with Python code and output inline below each cell).](data:image/svg+xml;base64,){fig-align="center" width="90%" fig-alt="Jupyter Notebook editor showing a notebook with markdown headings, Python code cells, and charts displayed inline."}

**Anatomy of the notebook:** ① **Menu bar** (File / Edit / Cell / Kernel / Help). ② **Toolbar** (▶ Run, ⬛ Stop, ➕ Insert). ③ **Cell** — blue border = selected in command mode; green border = edit mode. ④ **Output** — plots and text appear directly below the code that generated them.
:::

#### Method 1 — Anaconda Navigator (GUI — easiest for beginners)

::: step-box
[1]{.step-num} Open **Anaconda Navigator** from the Start Menu (Windows) or Applications (macOS)

[2]{.step-num} Find the **Jupyter Notebook** tile and click **Launch**

[3]{.step-num} Your browser opens automatically at `http://localhost:8888/tree`

[4]{.step-num} Navigate to your project folder → click **New → Python 3** to create a notebook
:::

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## 🖼️ Anaconda Navigator — What You Will See

![Anaconda Navigator home screen showing the Jupyter Notebook and JupyterLab launch tiles.](data:image/svg+xml;base64,){fig-align="center" width="90%" fig-alt="Anaconda Navigator home screen with application tiles including Jupyter Notebook, JupyterLab, Spyder, and VS Code."}

> **Key areas:** ① Home tab shows available applications. ② **Environments** tab (left sidebar) lets you switch conda environments. ③ Click the ▶ **Launch** button on any tile to open that tool.
:::

#### Method 2 — Command Line

```{bash launch_jupyter}
conda activate stats_project                   # Activate your environment
jupyter notebook                               # Launch Jupyter
jupyter notebook --notebook-dir=~/Documents    # Or specify a folder
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Cells, Kernels, and Output

#### Cell Types

| Cell Type | Purpose | Keyboard shortcut to switch |
|----|----|----|
| **Code** | Python that runs | Press `Y` in command mode |
| **Markdown** | Formatted text, headings, equations | Press `M` in command mode |
| **Raw** | Plain text (not rendered) | Press `R` in command mode |

#### ⌨️ Essential Keyboard Shortcuts

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## ⌨️ Jupyter Shortcuts Every Statistician Should Know

| Shortcut            | Action                           |
|---------------------|----------------------------------|
| `Shift + Enter`     | ▶ Run cell, move to next         |
| `Ctrl + Enter`      | ▶ Run cell, stay here            |
| `Esc`               | Enter command mode (blue border) |
| `Enter`             | Enter edit mode (green border)   |
| `A` (command mode)  | Insert cell **above**            |
| `B` (command mode)  | Insert cell **below**            |
| `DD` (command mode) | Delete current cell              |
| `M` (command mode)  | Cell → Markdown                  |
| `Y` (command mode)  | Cell → Code                      |
| `Z` (command mode)  | Undo cell deletion               |
| `Shift + M`         | Merge selected cells             |
:::

#### Magic Commands — Special Powers

Magic commands start with `%` (line magic) or `%%` (cell magic):

```{python magics}
# ── Display matplotlib plots inline (run once at top of notebook)
%matplotlib inline
%config InlineBackend.figure_format = 'retina'   # for crisp charts on high-resolution screens

# ── Time how long a computation takes
%timeit sum(range(1_000_000))

# ── Time an entire cell
%%timeit
import numpy as np
np.linalg.eig(np.random.randn(500, 500))

# ── See all variables in memory and their types
%whos

# ── Reload external Python files you have edited
%load_ext autoreload
%autoreload 2

# ── Run an external Python script inside your notebook
%run my_analysis_script.py

# ── List all available magic commands
%lsmagic
```

#### The Kernel

::: {.callout-important icon="false"}
## 🔁 Always "Restart & Run All" before submitting or sharing

The **kernel** is the Python process behind your notebook. Running cells out of order is a common source of errors. Before sharing your notebook, go to:

**Kernel → Restart & Run All Cells**

This re-runs every cell from top to bottom and confirms true reproducibility.
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## JupyterLab

### What is JupyterLab?

**JupyterLab** is the next-generation interface — a full IDE experience in your browser. It is the recommended interface for most users today.

::::::::::: concept-grid
:::: concept-pill
::: icon
🗂️
:::

**Tabbed layout**\
Multiple notebooks open at once
::::

:::: concept-pill
::: icon
📁
:::

**File browser**\
Left sidebar file explorer
::::

:::: concept-pill
::: icon
💻
:::

**Built-in terminal**\
Run shell commands without leaving browser
::::

:::: concept-pill
::: icon
✏️
:::

**Text editor**\
Edit `.py`, `.csv`, `.md` files directly
::::
:::::::::::

### Launching JupyterLab

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## 🖼️ JupyterLab — Interface Overview

![JupyterLab interface showing the left sidebar file browser, a notebook tab open in the main work area.](data:image/svg+xml;base64,){fig-align="center" width="90%" fig-alt="JupyterLab browser interface with left sidebar showing file browser, main notebook editor area, and tab bar at the top."}

**Key areas of JupyterLab:** ① **Left sidebar** — file browser, running kernels, extensions. ② **Main work area** — drag and drop tabs to tile notebooks side by side. ③ **Launcher** (+ tab) — quickly create notebooks, terminals, or open a text editor. ④ **Status bar** (bottom) — shows the active kernel and Python environment.

> 💡 **Tip for beginners:** JupyterLab looks more complex than Jupyter Notebook but works the same way at the notebook level. The extra panels (terminal, file editor, multiple tabs) become very useful as projects grow.
:::

```{bash launch_jupyterlab}
conda activate stats_project
jupyter lab           # Opens at http://localhost:8888/lab
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Other IDEs

::::::: panel-tabset
## 💻 VSCode

**Visual Studio Code** is a free, lightweight, highly extensible editor from Microsoft.

::: step-box
[1]{.step-num} Download from [**https://code.visualstudio.com**](https://code.visualstudio.com)

[2]{.step-num} Install the **Python** extension (by Microsoft)

[3]{.step-num} Install the **Jupyter** extension (by Microsoft)

[4]{.step-num} Open a `.ipynb` file — it renders as an interactive notebook

[5]{.step-num} Select interpreter: `Ctrl+Shift+P` → **Python: Select Interpreter** → choose your conda env
:::

```{bash vscode_cli}
# Install extensions from terminal
code --install-extension ms-python.python
code --install-extension ms-toolsai.jupyter
code --install-extension ms-python.pylance
```

**Best for:** Working on `.py` scripts and notebooks side-by-side, larger codebases.

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## 🖼️ VS Code — Python & Jupyter Interface

![VS Code editor with a Jupyter notebook (.ipynb) open in the main panel.](data:image/svg+xml;base64,){fig-align="center" width="90%" fig-alt="VS Code editor showing a Jupyter notebook with Python code cells, inline plot output, and variable explorer panel."}

**Key areas:** ① **Activity Bar** (far left) — switch between Explorer, Search, Git, Extensions. ② **Notebook editor** (centre) — identical cell-based editing to Jupyter Notebook. ③ **Variable Explorer** (top right in notebook mode) — inspect live variables without extra code. ④ **Kernel selector** (top right, e.g. "Python 3.11 (stats_project)") — click to change conda environment.
:::

## 🔴 PyCharm

**PyCharm Community** (free) by JetBrains is a professional Python IDE.

| Feature           | VSCode              | PyCharm Community             |
|-------------------|---------------------|-------------------------------|
| Cost              | Free                | Free                          |
| Jupyter in-editor | ✅ Excellent        | ⚠️ Basic                      |
| Code debugging    | ✅ Good             | ⭐ Best-in-class              |
| Code refactoring  | ✅ Good             | ⭐ Best-in-class              |
| Best for          | Notebooks + scripts | Large packages & applications |

Download from: <https://www.jetbrains.com/pycharm/>

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## 🖼️ PyCharm — Python IDE Interface

![PyCharm Community Edition IDE showing a Python script in the editor with syntax highlighting.](data:image/svg+xml;base64,){fig-align="center" width="90%" fig-alt="PyCharm IDE with Python script, project navigator on left, run panel at bottom showing output, and code completion popup."}

**Key areas:** ① **Project tree** (left) — all files in your project folder. ② **Editor** (centre) — smart code completion, error highlighting, and refactoring. ③ **Run / Debug tool window** (bottom) — output, errors, interactive console. ④ **Python Console** tab — type Python interactively, similar to IPython. ⑤ **Interpreter selector** (bottom right) — click to switch conda environments.
:::

## ☁️ Google Colab

**Google Colaboratory** is a free, cloud-hosted Jupyter environment — **no installation required**.

Access at: [**https://colab.research.google.com**](https://colab.research.google.com)

| Feature         | Value               |
|-----------------|---------------------|
| Installation    | None — browser only |
| Cost            | Free (with limits)  |
| GPU/TPU         | ✅ Free access      |
| Storage         | Google Drive        |
| Session timeout | \~12 hours (free)   |
| Sensitive data  | ⚠️ Not suitable     |

```{python colab_example}
# Mount Google Drive for persistent storage
from google.colab import drive
drive.mount('/content/drive')

import pandas as pd
df = pd.read_csv('/content/drive/MyDrive/data/my_dataset.csv')
df.describe()
```

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## 🖼️ Google Colab — Browser Interface

![Google Colab notebook interface open in a Chrome browser tab.](data:image/svg+xml;base64,){fig-align="center" width="90%" fig-alt="Google Colab logo."}

**How Colab compares to local Jupyter:**

| Feature | Google Colab | Local Jupyter/JupyterLab |
|----|----|----|
| **Start time** | Instant (browser) | \~10 seconds (conda activate + jupyter lab) |
| **Free GPU** | ✅ Yes (T4/V100) | ❌ Depends on your hardware |
| **Works offline** | ❌ Requires internet | ✅ Fully offline |
| **Persistent files** | Via Google Drive only | Local disk (always available) |
| **Sensitive data** | ⚠️ Not recommended | ✅ Stays on your machine |
| **Package install** | `!pip install` per session | `conda install` once per env |

> **How to get started:** Go to [colab.research.google.com](https://colab.research.google.com), sign in with a Google account, and click **New notebook**. Your notebook is saved automatically to Google Drive.
:::

## 🖥️ Terminal / REPL

For production work, automation, and server-side analysis:

```{bash terminal_usage}
# Run a Python script directly
python analysis.py

# Run with arguments
python analysis.py --input data.csv --output results.csv

# Interactive Python REPL
python

# IPython — enhanced REPL (included with Anaconda)
ipython

# Run a one-liner
python -c "import scipy; print(scipy.__version__)"
```

| Use Jupyter/JupyterLab      | Use the terminal                        |
|-----------------------------|-----------------------------------------|
| Exploratory data analysis   | Running finished analysis automatically |
| Teaching or presenting      | Scheduled (cron) jobs                   |
| Sharing interactive reports | Processing data on a server             |
| Learning Python             | CI/CD pipelines                         |
:::::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Putting It All Together — Your First Statistical Session

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## 🚀 Complete Workflow: Launch → Analyse → Visualise

Follow these steps to run your first real statistical analysis in Python.
:::

### Step 1 — Launch your environment

```{bash first_session_launch}
conda activate stats_project    # Activate your environment
cd ~/Documents/stats_project    # Navigate to your project
jupyter lab                     # Open JupyterLab
```

### Step 2 — Set up your notebook

```{python first_cell_imports}
# Cell 1: Import all libraries
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import scipy.stats as stats
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid", palette="deep")
%matplotlib inline
%config InlineBackend.figure_format = 'retina'

print("✅ Libraries loaded successfully!")
print(f"   numpy   {np.__version__}")
print(f"   pandas  {pd.__version__}")
```

### Step 3 — Create data and run a t-test

```{python first_cell_ttest}
# Cell 2: Simulate two groups and run an independent t-test
np.random.seed(2024)
control   = np.random.normal(loc=50, scale=10, size=30)
treatment = np.random.normal(loc=56, scale=10, size=30)

t_stat, p_value = stats.ttest_ind(control, treatment)
print(f"t-statistic = {t_stat:.3f}")
print(f"p-value     = {p_value:.4f}")
print("→ Statistically significant!" if p_value < 0.05 else "→ Not significant at α = 0.05")
```

### Step 4 — Visualise

```{python first_cell_plot}
# Cell 3: Boxplot + strip plot
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 5))

# Left: Boxplot
axes[0].boxplot([control, treatment], labels=["Control", "Treatment"],
                patch_artist=True,
                boxprops=dict(facecolor="#dbeafe", color="#1d4ed8"),
                medianprops=dict(color="#dc2626", linewidth=2))
axes[0].set_ylabel("Score")
axes[0].set_title(f"Independent t-test\nt = {t_stat:.2f},  p = {p_value:.4f}")

# Right: Distributions
x = np.linspace(20, 90, 300)
for grp, col, lbl in [(control,"#3b82f6","Control"),(treatment,"#ef4444","Treatment")]:
    mu, sd = grp.mean(), grp.std(ddof=1)
    axes[1].plot(x, stats.norm.pdf(x, mu, sd), linewidth=2.5, label=f"{lbl} (μ={mu:.1f})", color=col)
    axes[1].hist(grp, bins=12, density=True, alpha=0.25, color=col)
axes[1].set_xlabel("Score")
axes[1].set_ylabel("Density")
axes[1].set_title("Overlapping Distributions")
axes[1].legend()

plt.suptitle("First Statistical Analysis in Python", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold", y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Summary and Next Steps

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## ✅ Modules 1–3 at a Glance

```{r summary_final, echo=FALSE, eval=TRUE}
df_sum <- data.frame(
  Module = c("1 · What is Python?","2 · Installation & Setup","3 · Python Environment"),
  `Core Message` = c(
    "Python 3 is free, readable, interpreted. Complements R; replaces SPSS/MATLAB for most tasks.",
    "Use Anaconda. conda manages packages & envs. One environment per project. Verify with conda --version.",
    "Jupyter/JupyterLab for analysis. VSCode for scripting. Colab for quick cloud work. Restart kernel before sharing."
  ),
  `Statistical Value` = c(
    "Superior to SPSS for reproducibility; equal to R for stats; better than R for ML pipelines.",
    "scipy, statsmodels, pandas install in one conda command. No manual library management.",
    "Notebook format = statistical report + code + output in one file. Ideal for reproducible research."
  ),
  check.names = FALSE
)
knitr::kable(df_sum, caption="Modules 1–3 Summary")
```
:::

### 📚 References

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## 📖 Recommended Reading

1.  **VanderPlas, J.** (2016). *Python Data Science Handbook: Essential Tools for Working with Data*. O'Reilly.\
    🌐 Free online: <https://jakevdp.github.io/PythonDataScienceHandbook/>

2.  **Rogel-Salazar, J.** (2017). *Data Science and Analytics with Python*. CRC Press / Chapman and Hall.

3.  **Python Software Foundation.** <https://www.python.org>

4.  **Anaconda Inc.** <https://www.anaconda.com/download>

5.  **Project Jupyter.** <https://jupyter.org>
:::

### 🔜 Coming Up Next

| Module | Topics |
|----|----|
| **Python Basics** | Variables, data types, operators, control flow (`if`/`for`/`while`), functions, data structures (list/dict/tuple/set), modules & imports |
| **Key Libraries** | NumPy arrays & maths · pandas DataFrames · matplotlib/seaborn visualisation · SciPy statistical tests · statsmodels regression |
| **Capstone Project** | Load a real CSV → descriptive stats → hypothesis test → visualisation → Quarto report |

------------------------------------------------------------------------
